note_data.title: Medical Physics
keyPoints: **X-rays** are electromagnetic radiation with wavelengths of approximately 10^-10 m that can penetrate soft tissue but are absorbed by bone
**Ultrasound** uses high frequency sound waves (1-15 MHz) that reflect at boundaries between tissues of different acoustic impedance
**PET scans** detect gamma rays emitted by positron-emitting radioisotopes to produce images of metabolic activity
**MRI** uses strong magnetic fields and radiofrequency pulses to align and excite hydrogen nuclei in water molecules
**Acoustic impedance** Z = ρc where ρ is density and c is speed of sound in the medium
**Attenuation** of X-rays follows I = I₀e^(-μx) where μ is the linear attenuation coefficient
**Half-value thickness** is the thickness of material that reduces X-ray intensity to half its original value
**Doppler ultrasound** can measure blood flow velocity using the frequency shift of reflected waves
**Contrast agents** improve image quality by increasing the difference in absorption or reflection between tissues

definitions.definition: The product of density and speed of sound in a medium, measured in kg m^-2 s^-1
A measure of how easily X-rays penetrate a material, with units m^-1
The thickness of material required to reduce the intensity of X-rays to half the incident intensity
An imaging technique that detects gamma rays from positron-electron annihilation to map metabolic activity
The property of certain crystals to produce an electric field when mechanically stressed or vice versa

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
State similarities AND differences between imaging techniques
Give a reasonable proposal with scientific justification

modelAnswer.question: Explain why ultrasound imaging requires a coupling gel between the transducer and skin. [3 marks]
modelAnswer.answer: Air has a very low acoustic impedance compared to skin. Without gel, there would be almost total reflection at the air-skin boundary due to the large impedance mismatch. The coupling gel has an acoustic impedance similar to skin, allowing ultrasound waves to enter the body with minimal reflection.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Air has low acoustic impedance / large impedance difference
Mark 2: Total reflection occurs at air-skin boundary
Mark 3: Gel has similar impedance to skin / reduces reflection

commonMistakes: Never say 'X-rays are light waves' — always say 'X-rays are electromagnetic radiation'
Students often confuse ultrasound frequency with X-ray frequency — ultrasound is MHz, X-rays are much higher
Don't write 'bones are white on X-rays because they are hard' — say 'bones appear white because they have high linear attenuation coefficient'
Never say 'ultrasound bounces off organs' — always say 'ultrasound reflects at boundaries between tissues'
Students forget that PET scans detect gamma rays, not positrons directly
examinerTips: Medical physics appears frequently in Paper 4 as a practical context question
Always quote the attenuation formula when discussing X-ray absorption
Draw ray diagrams for ultrasound reflection to show your understanding
Remember to state units for acoustic impedance (kg m^-2 s^-1)
Link physics principles to medical applications for full marks
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: X-ray attenuation graph showing exponential decay of intensity with thickness, and ultrasound reflection diagram showing incident, reflected and transmitted waves at tissue boundaries with different acoustic impedances
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to explain how ultrasound is used in medical imaging and calculate the depth of a reflected sound from given time and speed data
N 2022 P2 Q5 (5 marks) tested X-ray production, asking candidates to describe how X-rays are generated in an X-ray tube and explain why a lead apron protects medical staff
M/J 2022 P1 Q12 (1 mark) was a multiple choice on the frequency range of ultrasound used in medical diagnosis
N 2023 P2 Q3 (4 marks) required students to compare the advantages and disadvantages of using X-rays versus ultrasound for medical imaging
M/J 2021 P2 Q6 (7 marks) tested understanding of how fiber optics work in endoscopes and asked for calculations involving critical angle

workedExample.question: An ultrasound pulse is transmitted into a patient's body and the reflected pulse is detected 0.26 ms later. The speed of ultrasound in body tissue is 1500 m/s. Calculate the depth of the reflecting surface. (3 marks)
steps: Step 1: Identify that the pulse travels to the reflecting surface AND back, so total distance = 2 × depth
Step 2: Use equation: distance = speed × time = 1500 m/s × 0.26 × 10⁻³ s = 0.39 m
Step 3: Calculate depth = total distance ÷ 2 = 0.39 m ÷ 2 = 0.195 m = 19.5 cm
workedExample.answer: The ultrasound travels to the reflecting surface and back, so total distance = speed × time = 1500 × 0.26 × 10⁻³ = 0.39 m. Therefore depth = 0.39 ÷ 2 = 0.195 m or 19.5 cm
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark: Recognition that pulse travels twice the depth; 1 mark: Correct substitution into distance equation; 1 mark: Final answer with correct unit

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates failed to recognize that ultrasound pulses travel to the target and back' — always remember to divide the calculated distance by 2 when finding depth using pulse-echo timing
Examiner Report 2022: 'Students often confused X-ray absorption with reflection' — clearly state that X-rays are absorbed differently by different tissues, not reflected like ultrasound

Examiner Report 2023: 'Candidates frequently gave incomplete explanations of total internal reflection in fiber optics' — always mention that light travels down the core by total internal reflection at the core-cladding boundary when the angle exceeds the critical angle
note_data.keyDiagram: Essential X-ray tube diagram showing: heated cathode filament (source of electrons), high voltage between cathode and anode (typically 50-100 kV), tungsten target anode, X-rays emerging at right angles to electron beam, lead shielding around tube with small exit window. Must label electron path, heat production at anode, and indicate that only 1% of energy becomes X-rays while 99% becomes heat.
note_data.quickRecap: Medical physics covers three key imaging methods: X-rays (absorbed differently by tissues, good for bones), ultrasound (reflected at tissue boundaries, safe for pregnancy), and fiber optic endoscopes (use total internal reflection). Remember ultrasound calculations need dividing by 2 for depth. X-rays are produced when high-speed electrons hit a metal target, creating mainly heat with some X-ray photons.
